prompt: Are AI fashion design tools leading to creative homogenization in the industry? How can the copyright disputes between independent designers and algorithms be resolved?
article:
## 1) What “AI fashion design tools” are actually doing in 2023–2026 (and why that matters for homogenization)

### A. GenAI for *ideation* (2D concept images, moodboards, “collection directions”)
- General-purpose image generators (Midjourney, Stable Diffusion, DALL·E) are widely used to rapidly visualize concepts and variations, but outputs typically still require significant human refinement and do not directly translate into production-ready garments; IP/legal uncertainty is repeatedly flagged as a core risk area for brands using these tools in design workflows [[1]](https://textiles.ncsu.edu/news/2024/06/heres-how-the-fashion-industry-is-using-ai/).  
- **CALA** embedded DALL·E-based generation into a supply-chain-connected workflow: users pick garment templates (e.g., blouse), provide text attributes, generate multiple variants, “regenerate,” iterate collaboratively, and then move toward production inside the same platform [[2]](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/powered-by-dall-es-ai-system-cala-makes-it-possible-to-generate-apparel-accessory-shoe-and-lifestyle-product-designs-from-natural-language-descriptions-and-reference-images-301654308.html). This “template + prompt + regenerate” structure is a recurring pattern that can either accelerate exploration or—if everyone uses the same templates and prompt tropes—narrow outputs.

### B. 3D/Digital Product Creation (DPC) pipelines that connect design → development → selling assets
Even when not branded as “GenAI,” these pipelines shape what’s feasible to produce (and thus how diverse the final product space can be).
- **Tapestry (Coach/Kate Spade)** describes a “create once, use many” DPC pipeline using Rhino/CLO 3D/ZBrush plus Adobe Substance 3D and Illustrator/Photoshop integration to accelerate approvals and reduce physical prototyping; digital twins are reused downstream in merchandising/marketing/retail activations [[8]](https://blog.adobe.com/en/publish/2025/10/03/how-tapestry-scaling-fashion-design-digital-twins-substance-3d).  
- **CLO** introduced “CLO AI Studio” as a centralized hub for AI-powered features, alongside tools like 3D Pen that convert 3D silhouettes into 2D patterns—further compressing the iteration cycle from concept to pattern [[10]](https://www.clo3d.com/en/resources/notices/1087). CLO’s APIs (e.g., BOM export) reinforce that these systems are increasingly automated and integrated into production toolchains [[11]](https://developer.clo3d.com/changelog.html).  
- **Browzwear + Lalaland.ai** integrates “hyperreal customizable AI models” into the 3D workflow so brands can validate and present designs on diverse synthetic models through DPC and go-to-market; Browzwear also launched “AI Models” (custom and in-product libraries) emphasizing privacy-by-design and brand exclusivity [[12]](https://browzwear.com/blog/vstitcher-and-lalaland-integration-2023) [[14]](https://help.browzwear.com/en/articles/13065171-ai-models-release-notes).  

### C. AI for *marketing and merchandising imagery* (digital twins, synthetic models, generative campaigns)
These uses don’t directly design garments, but they influence what aesthetics get rewarded and scaled.
- **Casablanca** used Midjourney-generated campaign imagery (SS23) built via a creative process that still involved planning, storyboarding, and photographing garments on models for pose references—reducing some production steps but not eliminating human creative direction [[3]](https://vmagazine.com/article/casablanca-paris-crosses-the-threshold-into-artificial-intelligence/) [[4]](https://hypebeast.com/2023/5/casablanca-futuro-optimisto-spring-summer-2023-ai-generated-campaign).  
- **Levi’s** announced tests of AI-generated models (via Lalaland.ai) to supplement human models, framed as enabling more diverse representation per product while explicitly stating it would not replace live shoots; it later clarified earlier DEI framing after backlash [[5]](https://www.levistrauss.com/2023/03/22/lsco-partners-with-lalaland-ai/).  
- **H&M** released images featuring digital twins and framed GenAI as a way to “amplify and elevate” a human-centric creative process [[6]](https://hmgroup.com/news/hm-continues-its-exploration-of-creativity-with-ai/).  
- **Zara/Inditex** was reported to use GenAI to accelerate catalog imagery and roll out an AI-based virtual fitting room; the report also describes consent/compensation practices for models whose images were AI-edited (note: single-publication reporting; treat rollout scope cautiously) [[7]](https://mexicobusiness.news/ecommerce/news/zara-deploys-generative-ai-models-virtual-fitting-rooms).

### D. AI trend forecasting and “buyer intelligence” as upstream control knobs on creativity
- **Heuritech** positions AI/computer vision as scanning millions of social images, extracting attributes, clustering trends, and forecasting trajectories; it describes weekly analysis of millions of images and productized trend calendars/forecasts [[77]](https://heuritech.com/articles/fashion-forecasting/) [[78]](https://heuritech.com/market-reports/trend-calendar-2025/). Wallpaper* reported luxury brands (e.g., Dior, Prada, Louis Vuitton) using Heuritech insights in planning contexts (reported, not primary brand documentation) [[17]](https://www.wallpaper.com/fashion-beauty/is-ai-the-future-of-fashion-trend-forecasting).  
- **WGSN** launched buyer-focused products combining human forecasting with AI (TrendCurve AI) and introduced TikTok Analytics to forecast trend lifespan beyond raw virality; BoF reports WGSN’s ML classifies trends using data from social/runway/retail and weights factors differently by trend type [[18]](https://www.wgsn.com/en/wgsn/press/press-releases/wgsn-launches-fashion-buying-dedicated-intelligence-and-forecast-led) [[19]](https://sourcingjournal.com/denim/denim-business/wgsn-fashion-buying-new-platform-retailers-trend-forecasting-ai-tiktok-521045/) [[79]](https://www.businessoffashion.com/news/retail/wgsn-launches-ai-driven-platform-for-buyers/) [[80]](https://www.wgsn.com/en/wgsn/press/press-releases/how-and-when-back-viral-trend-wgsn-launches-tiktok-analytics).

**Why this tooling landscape matters:** homogenization, if it happens, will rarely come from a single “AI design app.” It emerges from *system-level coupling*—many brands drawing from the same foundation models, the same trend-intelligence layers, the same engagement-optimized platforms, and increasingly automated production pipelines.

---

## 2) Are AI fashion design tools leading to creative homogenization?

### Bottom line
**There is credible evidence for homogenization pressures and convergence mechanisms, but limited direct “industry-wide causal proof” that GenAI has already made runway/retail fashion uniformly more similar.** The best-supported conclusion is:

- **AI tools can increase individual productivity/creative quality while simultaneously increasing group-level similarity** (collective novelty declines) in creative domains [[25]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38996021/) [[30]](https://barrettrees.com/papers/Homogenization_CHI2024.pdf).  
- In fashion specifically, **the combination of shared models + shared prompt conventions + shared trend signals + faster supply chains** creates strong conditions for convergence—even if individual designers feel more creative.

So: **Yes, there is a meaningful homogenization risk, and in some adjacent domains it is already measurable; in fashion it is best described as an emerging, structurally likely effect rather than a fully quantified industry outcome.**

---

## 3) Mechanisms that drive homogenization (with evidence you can actually measure)

### A. “Shared-model, shared-dataset” convergence (data monoculture)
If many designers use the same few foundation models trained on similar internet-scale corpora, outputs drift toward the same high-probability aesthetic basins.
- Fashion scholarship explicitly warns of **bias and homogenization** if many designers adopt the same algorithmic tools; the “aesthetic palette” can be constrained by training data, creating a **“data monoculture”** that sidelines vernacular/subcultural styles [[76]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2507.17758) (similar argument also appears in related fashion AI discourse [[33]](https://hal.sorbonne-universite.fr/hal-05056553v1/file/Weaving%20the%20Future_Sorbonne_ARTICLE.pdf)).  
- Text-to-image systems have documented **representation biases** and under-representation of marginalized identities; the bias sources include web data, “aesthetic” filtering, CLIP guidance, and safety filters—parts of the pipeline that can systematically steer outputs toward a narrow notion of “good taste” [[31]](https://papers.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/b01153e7112b347d8ed54f317840d8af-Paper-Datasets_and_Benchmarks.pdf). Bias in bodies/beauty norms is also highlighted in broader surveys of generative image bias (including body-type bias) [[32]](https://arxiv.org/html/2407.01556v1).  

**Fashion implication:** if the generative substrate encodes a narrow “editorial beauty” prior, then even diverse prompts can yield a recognizable “platform look,” especially in campaign imagery, model renderings, and early concept boards.

**How to measure it in fashion pipelines**
- Run prompt audits across identity markers and style descriptors; cluster generated images and quantify representation diversity, using methods like clustering-based evaluation and controlled prompt variation audits [[31]](https://papers.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2023/file/b01153e7112b347d8ed54f317840d8af-Paper-Datasets_and_Benchmarks.pdf).  
- Track silhouette/color/material distributions in GenAI concept outputs vs historical brand archives (do the tails shrink?).

---

### B. Prompt/template convergence and “regenerate until it looks right”
Fashion workflows increasingly use templates and iterative regeneration.
- CALA’s DALL·E feature is explicitly built around **product templates + natural-language attributes + regenerate loops** [[2]](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/powered-by-dall-es-ai-system-cala-makes-it-possible-to-generate-apparel-accessory-shoe-and-lifestyle-product-designs-from-natural-language-descriptions-and-reference-images-301654308.html).  
- Adobe’s Illustrator features (Text to Pattern, Generative Shape Fill) similarly turn prompts into repeatable, scalable design primitives (patterns, vectors) [[9]](https://news.adobe.com/news/news-details/2024/adobe-unveils-powerful-new-innovations-in-illustrator-and-photoshop-unlocking-new-design-possibilities-for-creative-pros).

**Homogenization pathway:** communities converge on the same prompt phrases (“editorial lighting,” “minimalist luxury,” “Paris street style,” etc.), the same negative prompts, and the same template silhouettes—especially under deadline pressure.

**How to measure it**
- Maintain a prompt registry and analyze prompt n-gram frequency over time; correlate with similarity in outputs (embedding similarity or perceptual similarity).  
- Apply the “homogenization analysis” approach proposed for creativity support tools: embed outputs and compute group-level similarity under different tool/prompt conditions [[30]](https://barrettrees.com/papers/Homogenization_CHI2024.pdf).

---

### C. Feedback loops: AI systems talking to AI systems (drift toward “visual elevator music”)
Even without humans, iterative generation loops can converge to generic motifs.
- A 2026 study of text→image→text→image loops (Stable Diffusion XL + LLaVA) found that **all runs converged to nearly identical visuals**, collapsing into **12 dominant, commercially safe motifs** (“visual elevator music”) across prompts and temperatures [[27]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12827715/).

**Fashion implication:** fashion teams increasingly chain tools—generate concepts → auto-caption → regenerate variations → select top performers → feed into marketing generation → feed into trend dashboards. If selection criteria are correlated (engagement, “on-brand,” “sellable”), the pipeline can drift toward a stable attractor set.

**How to measure it**
- Track “motif collapse” by clustering concept images across weeks/seasons and counting attractor motifs (similar to the motif-count framing in the convergence paper) [[27]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12827715/).

---

### D. Trend-forecasting AI as a coordination device (everyone chases the same signals)
AI forecasting systems reduce uncertainty; that can *also* reduce creative variance across brands if they all consume the same dashboards.
- Heuritech describes extracting attributes from millions of social images, clustering trends, and monitoring accelerations—then packaging those signals into forecasts [[77]](https://heuritech.com/articles/fashion-forecasting/) [[78]](https://heuritech.com/market-reports/trend-calendar-2025/).  
- WGSN’s buyer tools combine ML classification with runway/social/retail data and even TikTok “trend staying power” forecasts; BoF frames this as helping buyers decide what to phase in/out amid TikTok noise [[79]](https://www.businessoffashion.com/news/retail/wgsn-launches-ai-driven-platform-for-buyers/) [[80]](https://www.wgsn.com/en/wgsn/press/press-releases/how-and-when-back-viral-trend-wgsn-launches-tiktok-analytics).

**Homogenization pathway:** if dozens/hundreds of brands respond to the same quantified “animal print +67%” or “this TikTok aesthetic has staying power” signals, assortments converge—especially in mid-market and fast-fashion tiers.

**How to measure it**
- Compare cross-brand assortment similarity before/after adoption of shared forecasting platforms (color palettes, key silhouettes, prints).  
- Track timing synchronization: do many brands launch similar items within narrower windows?

---

### E. Recommender systems and “blockbuster drift” in fashion retail feeds
Even if designers create diverse items, algorithmic merchandising can narrow what consumers see and buy—feeding back into design.
- A WWW’24 simulation study finds that past-consumption-based recommendation can **reduce inter-user diversity** by homogenizing users toward “blockbuster” items—alleviating filter bubbles *by making everyone consume the same hits* [[85]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/155155/3589334.3645497.pdf).  
- Fashion-specific recommender systems are an active research/deployment area (visual recommendation, outfit recommendation, influencer-driven recs) [[86]](https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-3-031-22192-7), and fashion-tech journalism expects expanded AI “automated curation” and recommendation at scale [[87]](https://www.vogue.com/article/2025-in-fashion-tech-more-human-more-automated).

**Homogenization pathway:** sales signals become more concentrated; concentrated sales signals then drive forecasting and replenishment; the next season’s “what worked” narrows.

**How to measure it**
- Inter-user diversity of purchased styles (not just clicked items) pre/post recommender changes, using the paper’s diversity decomposition conceptually [[85]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/155155/3589334.3645497.pdf).

---

### F. Platform aesthetics and engagement optimization (the “Instagram face/body” problem)
Aesthetics that win engagement become the default training/conditioning target—especially for GenAI marketing.
- An Instagram-focused study of #AIphotography found highly interacted-with AI imagery disproportionately depicts women and reinforces specific youth/beauty norms; reverse-engineering prompts showed “toning down” toward more generic, realistic, and often whiter/younger depictions [[34]](https://www.digitalmethods.net/Dmi/WinterSchool2024AIphotography).

**Fashion implication:** if campaign ideation and social content generation are optimized for engagement, the output distribution can collapse toward a narrow “attention aesthetic,” influencing both brand imagery and product direction.

---

### G. Speed + scale (ultra-fast fashion) makes convergence visible in the market
Even modest convergence in trend signals becomes obvious when production volume is extreme.
- NIQ reports (EU e-shopper panel, 2022) **315,000 new items for Shein** vs thousands for Zara/H&M—illustrating how quickly detected aesthetics can be replicated at industrial scale [[84]](https://nielseniq.com/global/en/insights/analysis/2023/shein-zara-hm-close-up-on-the-ultra-fast-fashion-market/).  
- Industry commentary warns GenAI could reduce lead times dramatically and thus accelerate overproduction/overconsumption if used to increase cadence rather than reduce waste [[36]](https://www.vogue.com/article/why-fashion-should-think-carefully-about-using-generative-ai).

**Fashion implication:** AI doesn’t need to “invent sameness.” It just needs to reduce friction so the same few trend motifs propagate faster and wider.

---

## 4) Fashion-specific evidence: what we can and cannot claim today

### What is relatively strong (fashion-specific)
- A 2024 fashion image-mining study found **silhouette-attribute similarities across runway, influencer outfits, and online retailer “best items”** (2022 F/W), and that retailer best items were more similar to influencer outfits—evidence of diffusion/convergence across channels (not necessarily AI-caused, but consistent with an ecosystem where shared signals produce similarity) [[75]](https://fashionandtextiles.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40691-024-00394-8).  
- Fashion-focused academic discussion explicitly identifies homogenization risk through shared tools/training data (“data monoculture”) [[76]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2507.17758).

### What remains weak / open
- There is **no widely accepted, peer-reviewed “runway homogenization index”** attributing increased similarity across brands directly to GenAI adoption (2022–2026). Most strong causal evidence comes from adjacent creative domains (writing, ideation studies) and from general algorithmic systems research (recommenders), which is *highly relevant* but not fashion-runway causal proof [[25]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38996021/) [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S187118712500207X) [[30]](https://barrettrees.com/papers/Homogenization_CHI2024.pdf) [[85]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/155155/3589334.3645497.pdf).

---

## 5) Countervailing reality: AI can also increase novelty and *diversify* outcomes (under specific conditions)

### A. Empirical “tradeoff” pattern: quality up, collective diversity down
- In a controlled story-writing experiment, LLM help increased judged creativity and enjoyment, especially for weaker writers—but produced **more similar stories** across participants: a clear “individual benefit, collective-cost” dynamic [[25]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38996021/).  
- In an ideation study, AI support preserved thematic diversity but increased semantic convergence; it also produced some new categories and unique contributions, and early AI use could improve later originality/diversity even without AI—suggesting learning effects and path dependence [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S187118712500207X).  
- A user study proposes measuring homogenization explicitly; it found ChatGPT increased **group-level homogeneity** relative to a non-AI ideation tool [[30]](https://barrettrees.com/papers/Homogenization_CHI2024.pdf).

**Fashion translation:** AI can broaden access (more people can ideate), raise baseline output quality, and still compress the style distribution unless governance pushes exploration.

### B. “Creative accidents” and purposeful misuse can generate new fashion ideas
- The Acne Studios collaboration with Robbie Barrat is repeatedly cited as a case where AI outputs produced unusual, “alien” distortions that inspired novel runway details (e.g., odd openings) [[37]](https://xrgoespop.com/home/acne-studios-x-robbie-barrat).  
- Fashion designers using Stable Diffusion/Midjourney in early-stage visualization report that these tools can help them explore unconventional directions; they often judge outputs by emotional resonance rather than manufacturability at the inspiration stage [[28]](https://dl.designresearchsociety.org/context/drs-conference-papers/article/3319/viewcontent/680.pdf).

### C. Closed, brand-specific models can protect “design DNA” and reduce convergence
- Nike is described as using AI within a **closed system** and leveraging its digital archive to train AI on brand identity rather than relying on external internet imagery—explicitly contrasting internal generation with “images found online” workflows [[16]](https://www.domusweb.it/en/speciali/nike-designs-the-future/2024/computational-design-and-ai-nike-design-is-already-in-the-future.html).  
- Browzwear’s “Custom AI Models” emphasize brand-exclusive environments and no cross-client training/sharing—an architectural mitigation against everyone pulling from the same model aesthetics [[14]](https://help.browzwear.com/en/articles/13065171-ai-models-release-notes).

### D. Tooling that emphasizes transparency and “what-if” exploration supports divergence
- “FashionCook” (2025) proposes visual analytics for human–AI collaboration that provides transparent interpretations and what-if scenario exploration—exactly the kind of interface pattern that can preserve diversity by letting teams explore controlled alternatives rather than selecting a single “predicted best” design [[38]](https://www.computer.org/csdl/magazine/cg/2025/06/11142676/29wEi2eTk5i).

---

## 6) So—are AI fashion tools leading to homogenization? A balanced answer

### The strongest supported claim
**AI tools are not inherently homogenizing, but current industry incentives and default deployments make homogenization a likely emergent property**—especially via:
1) shared foundation models + prompt conventions [[2]](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/powered-by-dall-es-ai-system-cala-makes-it-possible-to-generate-apparel-accessory-shoe-and-lifestyle-product-designs-from-natural-language-descriptions-and-reference-images-301654308.html) [[9]](https://news.adobe.com/news/news-details/2024/adobe-unveils-powerful-new-innovations-in-illustrator-and-photoshop-unlocking-new-design-possibilities-for-creative-pros),  
2) shared AI forecasting and microtrend instrumentation [[77]](https://heuritech.com/articles/fashion-forecasting/) [[79]](https://www.businessoffashion.com/news/retail/wgsn-launches-ai-driven-platform-for-buyers/) [[80]](https://www.wgsn.com/en/wgsn/press/press-releases/how-and-when-back-viral-trend-wgsn-launches-tiktok-analytics),  
3) algorithmic recommendation/curation concentrating demand toward blockbusters [[85]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/155155/3589334.3645497.pdf) [[87]](https://www.vogue.com/article/2025-in-fashion-tech-more-human-more-automated),  
4) engagement-optimized platform aesthetics narrowing visual norms [[34]](https://www.digitalmethods.net/Dmi/WinterSchool2024AIphotography), and  
5) faster production scaling making convergence visible in retail [[84]](https://nielseniq.com/global/en/insights/analysis/2023/shein-zara-hm-close-up-on-the-ultra-fast-fashion-market/) [[36]](https://www.vogue.com/article/why-fashion-should-think-carefully-about-using-generative-ai).

### When homogenization is *most* likely
- Brands use the same general-purpose models “out of the box,”  
- prompts/templates are copied across teams,  
- success metrics are engagement or short-horizon sell-through,  
- trend tools become decision authorities rather than inputs, and  
- ultra-fast pipelines convert signals into product with minimal human critique.

### When AI is more likely to *increase* creative diversity
- Models are fine-tuned on brand archives or curated local/craft datasets [[16]](https://www.domusweb.it/en/speciali/nike-designs-the-future/2024/computational-design-and-ai-nike-design-is-already-in-the-future.html),  
- teams impose explicit “diversity constraints” (explore multiple aesthetic basins),  
- AI is used to generate *many* divergent directions and humans select intentionally (not just “what looks most platform-ready”), and  
- organizations measure similarity and reward novelty (treat homogenization as a monitored risk) [[30]](https://barrettrees.com/papers/Homogenization_CHI2024.pdf).

---

## 7) Copyright disputes between independent designers and algorithms: what the fights are actually about

Fashion IP is already structurally tricky (garment shapes often have limited copyright protection in some jurisdictions), so most disputes cluster around **(i) surface graphics/prints, (ii) brand identifiers, (iii) datasets/training, (iv) identity/likeness**.

### A. Training-data disputes (the “input side”)
- Visual artists sued Stability AI/Midjourney/DeviantArt alleging copyrighted works were copied into training datasets (LAION-related) and that models contain compressed/encoded representations enabling infringement; in Andersen v. Stability AI, the court allowed key copyright and inducement theories to proceed past dismissal stages, while DMCA §1202 claims were dismissed with prejudice in that case’s posture (as summarized in secondary reporting and reflected in the litigation record) [[47]](https://copyrightalliance.org/andersen-v-stability-ai-copyright-case/) [[96]](https://cdn.arstechnica.net/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/Andersen-v-Stability-AI-Order-on-Motions-to-Dismiss-8-12-2024.pdf).  
- Large rightsholders escalated: Disney and Universal sued Midjourney alleging it trained on copyrighted works and outputs infringe, characterizing the service as a “virtual vending machine” for unauthorized copies [[49]](https://www.npr.org/2025/06/12/nx-s1-5431684/ai-disney-universal-midjourney-copyright-infringement-lawsuit).  
- EU/German litigation highlights “memorisation” concerns: Munich I court in GEMA v OpenAI found near-verbatim lyric reproduction and accepted that training data can be embedded in model weights and later retrieved, ordering relief (first-instance; described in secondary analysis) [[58]](https://www.twobirds.com/en/insights/2025/landmark-ruling-of-the-munich-regional-court-(gema-v-openai)-on-copyright-and-ai-training).  
- In Germany, Kneschke v. LAION illustrates that **opt-outs must be machine-readable** under the statutory regime (as reported), rejecting mere natural-language website restrictions as insufficient for DSM Art. 4-style reservation mechanics [[59]](https://www.jdsupra.com/legalnews/robert-kneschke-v-laion-judgment-of-10-1561716/).

### B. Output similarity disputes (the “output side”)
- Getty v. Stability AI in the UK ultimately narrowed to secondary infringement theories around model weights and trademark/watermark issues; the High Court rejected the argument that weights were “infringing copies” because (as accepted by experts) weights did not contain copies of Getty images, but it made limited findings on trademark issues for outputs with watermark-like features in early SD versions (as described in detailed legal commentary) [[52]](https://www.twobirds.com/en/insights/2025/uk/stability-ai-defeats-getty-images-copyright-claims-in-first-of-its-kind-dispute-before-the-high-cour) [[53]](https://www.paulweiss.com/insights/client-memos/getty-images-v-stability-ai-the-uk-courts-first-word-on-use-of-copyright-works-in-ai-model-development) [[101]](https://katten.com/getty-images-v-stability-ai-intellectual-property-rights-in-the-age-of-generative-ai).  
- Output-side disputes are particularly relevant to fashion because generated imagery can accidentally include watermarks, logos, or brand trade dress elements in ads and lookbooks, triggering trademark and passing-off style claims [[52]](https://www.twobirds.com/en/insights/2025/uk/stability-ai-defeats-getty-images-copyright-claims-in-first-of-its-kind-dispute-before-the-high-cour) [[101]](https://katten.com/getty-images-v-stability-ai-intellectual-property-rights-in-the-age-of-generative-ai).

### C. “Algorithmic copying at scale” in fast fashion (independent designers vs platforms)
A distinct pattern is *not* “GenAI designing clothing,” but **algorithms/AI used to detect viral designs and reproduce them quickly.**
- Independent designers sued Shein alleging systematic, algorithm-driven copying and even used **civil RICO** theories. The 2023 complaint by Perry/Martinez/Baron alleged Shein’s “design algorithm” generates exact or close copies and that its AI selects designs with commercial potential [[91]](https://interactive.wusa9.com/pdfs/Shein.pdf).  
- A November 2024 order in that litigation discusses how criminal copyright infringement can serve as a RICO predicate and addresses pleading issues around willfulness and wire/mail fraud predicates in the alleged concealment scheme [[93]](https://ricoact.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/11/70-ORD-MTD2.pdf).  
- A 2024 class action (Alan Giana v. Shein) similarly alleges Shein uses algorithms/AI to scour for viral designs and transmit copied outputs directly to factories without human compliance review [[94]](https://www.classaction.org/media/giana-v-shein-distribution-corp-et-al.pdf) (also summarized in fashion-law commentary) [[50]](https://www.thefashionlaw.com/a-new-lawsuit-lifts-the-lid-on-sheins-ai-powered-ultra-fast-fashion-model/).

**Important nuance:** these are allegations, not adjudicated factual findings—yet they show how “algorithms” enter fashion copying disputes even without generative models.

### D. Who owns/protects AI-assisted fashion outputs?
- In the US, the Copyright Office’s guidance states copyright protects only human-authored material; AI-generated material must be disclaimed, and protection (if any) lies in human selection/arrangement or sufficiently creative modifications [[44]](https://www.copyright.gov/ai/ai_policy_guidance.pdf). USCO’s broader AI initiative reinforces a case-by-case approach centered on human authorship [[45]](https://copyright.gov/ai/) [[46]](https://www.copyright.gov/ai/Copyright-and-Artificial-Intelligence-Part-2-Copyrightability-Report.pdf).  
- In China, the Beijing Internet Court recognized copyright in an AI-generated image where the plaintiff showed substantial human intellectual input through prompt selection, parameter settings, and iterative curation in Stable Diffusion (described in detailed case summaries) [[67]](https://ipkitten.blogspot.com/2023/12/chinese-court-deems-ai-generated-image.html) [[68]](https://www.chinaiplawupdate.com/2023/11/beijing-internet-court-recognizes-copyright-in-ai-generated-images/).  
**Practical result:** independent designers face jurisdictional divergence: the same AI-assisted print might be protectable in China under certain facts but only partially protectable (human contributions only) in the US.

### E. Likeness/right-of-publicity: AI models and “digital twins”
Fashion increasingly uses digital replicas in marketing.
- New York’s Fashion Workers Act (effective June 19, 2025) requires **separate, specific written consent** to create/use a model’s digital replica (face/body/voice) that substantially replicates or replaces the model, excluding routine edits; it requires scope/purpose/pay/duration disclosures and provides enforcement mechanisms [[106]](https://www.beneschlaw.com/resources/seeing-double-new-york-fashion-workers-act-creates-new-consent-requirements-for-use-of-generative-ai-tools-to-create-models-digital-replicas.html) [[107]](https://www.cll.com/newsroom-news-173971) (see also additional summaries [[108]](https://www.wigdorlaw.com/legal-updates-insights/new-york-state-fashion-workers-act-becomes-law-thanks-to-sara-ziff-of-model-alliance/)).  
- Adjacent right-of-publicity litigation (e.g., AI voice cloning) shows trademark law often fails where the “identity” is the product; right-of-publicity statutes and consumer protection claims can do the work instead [[104]](https://www.blankrome.com/publications/breaking-down-intersection-right-publicity-law-ai) [[105]](https://www.loeb.com/en/insights/publications/2025/07/lehrman-v-lovo-inc). This matters for fashion if brands deploy synthetic spokesmodels or replicate real models.

---

## 8) How copyright disputes between independent designers and algorithms can be resolved (a realistic, multi-layer approach)

No single fix works because disputes arise at **three different layers**:
1) **Training data** (what went into the model),  
2) **Outputs** (what comes out and is commercialized), and  
3) **Market structure** (algorithmic trend-chasing and scale copying).

Below is a layered resolution framework that is both legally and operationally plausible in 2026.

---

# Layer 1 — Make training rights legible and scalable (opt-outs, licensing, transparency)

## 8.1 Standardize machine-readable opt-out/reservation signals (especially for independent creators)
The EU’s DSM Directive approach relies on machine-readable reservation of rights for TDM (Art. 4 logic), but practice has been fragmented. Solutions now exist:

### A. Implement TDMRep (W3C community protocol)
- TDMRep defines machine-readable “tdm-reservation” signals via a `/.well-known/tdmrep.json` file, explicitly designed as a technical response to DSM Directive constraints [[117]](https://www.w3.org/community/reports/tdmrep/CG-FINAL-tdmrep-20240510/).  
**Why it helps:** it creates a clearer “no training” signal than buried website terms.

### B. Follow IPTC’s 2025 opt-out best practices (pragmatic, multi-channel)
IPTC recommends a stack: visible rights notice, metadata tags, robots.txt, firewall bot blocking, TDMRep, trust.txt, IPTC Photo “Data Mining” metadata, and CAWG assertions inside C2PA-signed assets [[118]](https://iptc.org/std/guidelines/data-mining-opt-out/IPTC-Generative-AI-Opt-Out-Best-Practices.pdf).  
**Key practical insight:** IPTC explicitly notes many bots ignore robots.txt and that law doesn’t mandate any single standard; therefore it recommends HTTP-level blocking and layered signaling [[118]](https://iptc.org/std/guidelines/data-mining-opt-out/IPTC-Generative-AI-Opt-Out-Best-Practices.pdf).

### C. Use registry-based opt-out tooling where feasible (Spawning / “Do Not Train Registry”)
- “Have I Been Trained?” and Spawning’s Do Not Train Registry allow creators to search whether images appear in popular open datasets and register opt-outs; Stability AI and Hugging Face reportedly agreed to remove registered images before training new models (not universal; relies on voluntary compliance) [[133]](https://www.technologyreview.com/2024/11/21/1107108/four-ways-to-protect-your-art-from-ai/) [[134]](https://spawning.substack.com/p/the-spawning-guide-to-rights-reservations).  
**Limitations:** cannot undo prior training; not all model developers participate; operational availability can vary [[134]](https://spawning.substack.com/p/the-spawning-guide-to-rights-reservations) [[135]](https://haveibeentrained.com/).

**Resolution recommendation:** regulators and procurement contracts should treat compliance with recognized reservation protocols (e.g., IPTC/TDMRep + registry ingestion) as a baseline duty for model providers selling into the EU/UK.

---

## 8.2 Scale licensing beyond “every artist negotiates with every model developer”
Independent designers cannot negotiate individually with frontier model developers.

### A. Collective licensing (UK)
- UK licensing bodies (CLA / ALCS / PLS) announced a collective licence intended to pay authors for AI training uses and provide legal certainty for AI developers, aimed at rightsholders who can’t do direct deals [[115]](https://www.theguardian.com/books/2025/apr/23/collective-licence-to-ensure-uk-authors-get-paid-for-works-used-to-train-ai) [[116]](https://www.publishingscotland.org/2025/05/cla-pls-and-alcs-to-introduce-new-license-for-genai-training/).  
**Why it matters:** it’s a plausible middle layer between “unlicensed scraping” and impossible 1:1 negotiation.

### B. Extended collective licensing (ECL) proposals (Spain)
- Spain proposed an ECL mechanism for massive uses in developing general-purpose AI models, allowing representative CMOs to license with extended effect while preserving opt-out rights [[113]](https://communia-association.org/2024/12/10/a-first-look-at-the-spanish-proposal-to-introduce-ecl-for-ai-training/) [[114]](https://acrosslegal.com/en/ia/).  
**Key design tension:** proposals must avoid overlapping awkwardly with TDM exceptions and must clearly define whether ECL is a fallback when opt-outs are exercised [[113]](https://communia-association.org/2024/12/10/a-first-look-at-the-spanish-proposal-to-introduce-ecl-for-ai-training/).

### C. Transparency obligations under the EU AI Act (training content summaries)
- The EU AI Act requires GPAI providers to publish a public summary of training content; the AI Office published a Template intended to create a minimal baseline, listing categories such as scraped content, licensed datasets, user data, synthetic data, plus governance around copyright and opt-outs [[110]](https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/library/explanatory-notice-and-template-public-summary-training-content-general-purpose-ai-models) [[111]](https://regulations.ai/regulations/RAI-EU-NA-TPSTCXX-2025).  
- Commentary stresses the Template is high-level and may not identify specific works, limiting its usefulness for individual creators seeking proof or licensing leverage [[112]](https://www.williamfry.com/knowledge/eu-releases-ai-training-data-template/).

**Resolution recommendation:** pair high-level summaries (AI Act) with *auditable opt-out compliance* and *standardized licensing on-ramps* (collective licences / ECL). Transparency without a licensing path produces frustration; licensing without transparency produces mistrust.

---

# Layer 2 — Reduce output-side infringement and strengthen provenance evidence

## 8.3 Provenance standards for AI-generated and human-authored assets
Provenance doesn’t solve training legality, but it improves **accountability** and dispute handling for outputs used in commerce.

### A. C2PA / Content Credentials for origin and edit history
- C2PA provides an open provenance standard for tracing origin/history of media [[71]](https://spec.c2pa.org/). OpenAI’s DALL·E 3 adopted C2PA-style metadata and a visible “CR” mark (with known limitations: metadata can be stripped; screenshots remove it) [[124]](https://www.theverge.com/2024/2/6/24063954/ai-watermarks-dalle3-openai-content-credentials).  
- C2PA materials emphasize cryptographically signed, tamper-evident provenance and note it can be combined with watermarking for resilience [[122]](https://downloads.regulations.gov/NIST-2024-0001-0030/attachment_1.pdf).

### B. Watermarking/detection (SynthID)
- Google DeepMind’s SynthID watermarks AI-generated text/images/video with robustness caveats (not a silver bullet; effectiveness drops under heavy rewriting/translation) [[72]](https://deepmind.google/blog/watermarking-ai-generated-text-and-video-with-synthid/).

### C. EU AI Act transparency/marking direction
- EU AI Act transparency provisions (as summarized) push toward machine-detectable marking of AI-generated/manipulated content and deployer disclosure for deepfakes, with a draft code of practice discussing detectors, robustness, and provenance chain retention [[62]](https://www.kirkland.com/publications/kirkland-alert/2026/02/illuminating-ai-the-eus-first-draft-code-of-practice-on-transparency-for-ai).

**Resolution recommendation:** major fashion advertisers/retailers should require:
1) provenance credentials for synthetic campaign assets,
2) retention of generation logs (prompt + seed + model version) under legal hold policies, and
3) standardized disclosures for AI-altered model likenesses (plus NY-style consent compliance where applicable) [[106]](https://www.beneschlaw.com/resources/seeing-double-new-york-fashion-workers-act-creates-new-consent-requirements-for-use-of-generative-ai-tools-to-create-models-digital-replicas.html) [[107]](https://www.cll.com/newsroom-news-173971).

---

## 8.4 Output-side infringement prevention in brand workflows (practical controls)
Because fashion disputes often involve **prints/graphics/logos** and close copying, brands should implement:
- **Similarity screening** (reverse image search + embedding similarity) for AI-generated graphics before commercialization (aligns with the general embedding-similarity approach used in homogenization measurement) [[30]](https://barrettrees.com/papers/Homogenization_CHI2024.pdf).  
- **Trademark/logo detection** in generated images to prevent accidental brand sign reproduction (Getty’s watermark disputes illustrate why this matters) [[52]](https://www.twobirds.com/en/insights/2025/uk/stability-ai-defeats-getty-images-copyright-claims-in-first-of-its-kind-dispute-before-the-high-cour) [[101]](https://katten.com/getty-images-v-stability-ai-intellectual-property-rights-in-the-age-of-generative-ai).  
- **“No-reference” policies** for prompts that include competitor brand names or living artists’ names, plus auditing for “style of” usage (central in the Andersen litigation framing) [[96]](https://cdn.arstechnica.net/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/Andersen-v-Stability-AI-Order-on-Motions-to-Dismiss-8-12-2024.pdf).

---

# Layer 3 — Give independent designers workable enforcement and remedies (not just theories)

## 8.5 Clarify what is protectable and how to document authorship (especially in the US)
- USCO requires applicants to disclose AI-generated material and limits protection to human-authored contributions (selection/arrangement or modifications) [[44]](https://www.copyright.gov/ai/ai_policy_guidance.pdf).  

**Practical resolution step for independent designers:** maintain an “authorship dossier” for each work:
- concept sketches, iterative edits, layer files, human decisions, and evidence of transformation beyond the raw AI output. This is critical both for registration and later enforcement.

## 8.6 Use appropriately scaled dispute forums (not every designer can go to federal court)
- The US Copyright Claims Board (CCB) offers a lower-cost, voluntary alternative with capped damages and streamlined procedures (respondent can opt out) [[69]](https://ccb.gov/about/).  
This can be meaningful for small creators facing online copying of prints/graphics.

## 8.7 Platform/process remedies still matter (even if imperfect)
Even when training disputes are hard to litigate, output commercialization often happens on platforms (marketplaces, social ads). Fast takedown and repeat-infringer policies can reduce harm quickly—though the provided sources here focus more on litigation than on platform SOPs.

---

## 9) How to reduce homogenization *and* reduce copyright conflict at the same time (the strategic linkage)

These two issues are tightly connected: when brands fear copyright risk, they tend to use “safe” prompts and “generic” aesthetics—ironically increasing homogenization. So dispute resolution should be designed to *unlock* lawful specificity.

### A. “Licensed specificity” beats “generic safety”
If designers can legally use:
- licensed archives,
- licensed print libraries,
- culturally specific datasets with permissions,
then outputs can become **more diverse** while also lowering infringement risk.

Collective licensing and ECL proposals are important here because they make lawful training and lawful reference feasible at scale [[115]](https://www.theguardian.com/books/2025/apr/23/collective-licence-to-ensure-uk-authors-get-paid-for-works-used-to-train-ai) [[116]](https://www.publishingscotland.org/2025/05/cla-pls-and-alcs-to-introduce-new-license-for-genai-training/) [[113]](https://communia-association.org/2024/12/10/a-first-look-at-the-spanish-proposal-to-introduce-ecl-for-ai-training/) [[114]](https://acrosslegal.com/en/ia/).

### B. Brand-specific / closed models reduce convergence and disputes
Nike’s closed-system framing and reliance on internal archives illustrates a path: control training data, align outputs to brand identity, and reduce dependence on public scraped corpora [[16]](https://www.domusweb.it/en/speciali/nike-designs-the-future/2024/computational-design-and-ai-nike-design-is-already-in-the-future.html).  
Browzwear’s client-isolated AI model architecture similarly aims to preserve brand exclusivity [[14]](https://help.browzwear.com/en/articles/13065171-ai-models-release-notes).

### C. Measure homogenization as a governance KPI
Adopt explicit similarity metrics (embedding-based group-level similarity) and monitor drift over time [[30]](https://barrettrees.com/papers/Homogenization_CHI2024.pdf). This reframes homogenization from a vague cultural anxiety into an auditable risk like fit, quality, or return rate.

---

## 10) Concrete, actionable recommendations (by stakeholder)

### For independent designers (do-now checklist)
1) **Register protectable work** (especially surface graphics/prints) and document human authorship; disclose/limit AI material per USCO guidance if in the US [[44]](https://www.copyright.gov/ai/ai_policy_guidance.pdf).  
2) **Publish machine-readable training reservations**:
   - implement TDMRep `tdmrep.json` where you control the domain [[117]](https://www.w3.org/community/reports/tdmrep/CG-FINAL-tdmrep-20240510/);
   - use IPTC’s layered approach (robots.txt + metadata + firewall + C2PA/CAWG assertions where possible) [[118]](https://iptc.org/std/guidelines/data-mining-opt-out/IPTC-Generative-AI-Opt-Out-Best-Practices.pdf).  
3) **Use opt-out registries** where relevant (Spawning/DNTR), understanding limits [[133]](https://www.technologyreview.com/2024/11/21/1107108/four-ways-to-protect-your-art-from-ai/) [[134]](https://spawning.substack.com/p/the-spawning-guide-to-rights-reservations).  
4) **Embed provenance** in your own published work (C2PA where available) to support authenticity disputes later [[122]](https://downloads.regulations.gov/NIST-2024-0001-0030/attachment_1.pdf).  
5) **Enforce strategically**:
   - for clear copying of prints/graphics online, consider CCB in the US where appropriate [[69]](https://ccb.gov/about/);
   - preserve evidence (screenshots, product listings, timestamps, transaction records).

### For brands and retailers using AI tools
1) **Adopt a “training provenance policy”**: only use vendors who can (a) describe training sources under AI Act-style categories and (b) show opt-out compliance workflows [[111]](https://regulations.ai/regulations/RAI-EU-NA-TPSTCXX-2025) [[112]](https://www.williamfry.com/knowledge/eu-releases-ai-training-data-template/).  
2) **Contractual controls**:
   - require warranties about training legality and opt-out respect;
   - require audit rights and incident response.  
3) **Output controls**:
   - similarity screening for generated prints,
   - logo/watermark detection,
   - restrictions on artist-name prompts.  
4) **Consent compliance for digital twins** in jurisdictions like New York: separate written consent with scope/pay/duration for replicas; exclude routine edits only when truly routine [[106]](https://www.beneschlaw.com/resources/seeing-double-new-york-fashion-workers-act-creates-new-consent-requirements-for-use-of-generative-ai-tools-to-create-models-digital-replicas.html) [[107]](https://www.cll.com/newsroom-news-173971).  
5) **Diversity-by-design**:
   - use multiple models or curated fine-tunes rather than a single default model,
   - require teams to generate across multiple style basins,
   - measure similarity and reward novelty [[30]](https://barrettrees.com/papers/Homogenization_CHI2024.pdf).

### For AI model developers / platforms
1) **Operationalize opt-outs**: ingest TDMRep/IPTC signals and credible registries; publish compliance statements; log exclusion decisions [[117]](https://www.w3.org/community/reports/tdmrep/CG-FINAL-tdmrep-20240510/) [[118]](https://iptc.org/std/guidelines/data-mining-opt-out/IPTC-Generative-AI-Opt-Out-Best-Practices.pdf) [[134]](https://spawning.substack.com/p/the-spawning-guide-to-rights-reservations).  
2) **Transparency that is actually useful**: the AI Act template is a minimum; provide optional “domain inclusion checks” or creator query interfaces (even if not legally required) to reduce conflict escalation [[112]](https://www.williamfry.com/knowledge/eu-releases-ai-training-data-template/).  
3) **Anti-convergence mechanisms**: diversify decoding strategies, encourage exploration modes, and avoid default “commercially safe” collapse seen in autonomous loops [[27]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12827715/).  
4) **Output watermark/provenance**: support C2PA and robust watermarking with clear documentation of limitations [[124]](https://www.theverge.com/2024/2/6/24063954/ai-watermarks-dalle3-openai-content-credentials) [[72]](https://deepmind.google/blog/watermarking-ai-generated-text-and-video-with-synthid/).

### For policymakers / standards bodies
1) **Make “machine-readable reservation” concrete**: regulators should recognize a short list of acceptable protocols (e.g., TDMRep + specified metadata fields) to reduce uncertainty highlighted by litigation like Kneschke v. LAION [[59]](https://www.jdsupra.com/legalnews/robert-kneschke-v-laion-judgment-of-10-1561716/) [[117]](https://www.w3.org/community/reports/tdmrep/CG-FINAL-tdmrep-20240510/).  
2) **Pair transparency with remedies**: training summaries (EU AI Act) must be complemented by enforceable opt-out compliance and viable licensing routes; otherwise creators can’t act on the information [[111]](https://regulations.ai/regulations/RAI-EU-NA-TPSTCXX-2025) [[112]](https://www.williamfry.com/knowledge/eu-releases-ai-training-data-template/).  
3) **Support scalable licensing**: collective licensing and ECL experiments should be evaluated and refined to avoid overlap with exceptions and to ensure creator opt-out/compensation fairness [[115]](https://www.theguardian.com/books/2025/apr/23/collective-licence-to-ensure-uk-authors-get-paid-for-works-used-to-train-ai) [[116]](https://www.publishingscotland.org/2025/05/cla-pls-and-alcs-to-introduce-new-license-for-genai-training/) [[113]](https://communia-association.org/2024/12/10/a-first-look-at-the-spanish-proposal-to-introduce-ecl-for-ai-training/).

---

## 11) Open problems (what remains unresolved in 2026)
1) **Proof problem for training infringement:** even with training summaries, rightsholders may struggle to prove inclusion or unlawful copying at work-level granularity [[112]](https://www.williamfry.com/knowledge/eu-releases-ai-training-data-template/).  
2) **Style vs expression:** many harms are “style appropriation,” which is difficult to fit into copyright; plaintiffs often reach for trademark/trade dress/unfair competition theories (as seen in AI art litigation) [[96]](https://cdn.arstechnica.net/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/Andersen-v-Stability-AI-Order-on-Motions-to-Dismiss-8-12-2024.pdf).  
3) **Homogenization measurement in real fashion markets:** we have strong methods and adjacent-domain evidence, but limited large-scale, peer-reviewed causal measurement for runway/retail similarity attributable to GenAI specifically [[75]](https://fashionandtextiles.springeropen.com/articles/10.1186/s40691-024-00394-8) [[25]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38996021/) [[30]](https://barrettrees.com/papers/Homogenization_CHI2024.pdf).  
4) **Metadata stripping and provenance loss:** C2PA metadata can be removed in common sharing workflows; watermarking is imperfect; enforcement will remain probabilistic [[124]](https://www.theverge.com/2024/2/6/24063954/ai-watermarks-dalle3-openai-content-credentials) [[72]](https://deepmind.google/blog/watermarking-ai-generated-text-and-video-with-synthid/).  
5) **Global divergence on authorship/ownership:** US human-authorship strictness vs China’s recognition of sufficiently human-directed AI outputs complicates cross-border protection strategies [[44]](https://www.copyright.gov/ai/ai_policy_guidance.pdf) [[67]](https://ipkitten.blogspot.com/2023/12/chinese-court-deems-ai-generated-image.html) [[68]](https://www.chinaiplawupdate.com/2023/11/beijing-internet-court-recognizes-copyright-in-ai-generated-images/).

---

### Synthesis
- **Homogenization:** The best evidence indicates a real convergence risk driven by shared models, trend coordination systems, platform engagement aesthetics, and recommender/forecasting feedback loops—amplified by fast-fashion scale—yet AI can also increase novelty when used with curated data, closed systems, and explicit diversity governance [[76]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2507.17758) [[27]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12827715/) [[85]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/155155/3589334.3645497.pdf) [[84]](https://nielseniq.com/global/en/insights/analysis/2023/shein-zara-hm-close-up-on-the-ultra-fast-fashion-market/) [[16]](https://www.domusweb.it/en/speciali/nike-designs-the-future/2024/computational-design-and-ai-nike-design-is-already-in-the-future.html).  
- **Copyright disputes:** Resolution requires shifting from ad hoc conflict (lawsuits and PR crises) to *infrastructure*: standardized opt-outs (TDMRep/IPTC), scalable licensing (collective licences/ECL), transparency (AI Act training summaries), and provenance for outputs (C2PA/watermarks), plus accessible enforcement paths for small creators (CCB) and consent rules for digital replicas (NY) [[117]](https://www.w3.org/community/reports/tdmrep/CG-FINAL-tdmrep-20240510/) [[118]](https://iptc.org/std/guidelines/data-mining-opt-out/IPTC-Generative-AI-Opt-Out-Best-Practices.pdf) [[115]](https://www.theguardian.com/books/2025/apr/23/collective-licence-to-ensure-uk-authors-get-paid-for-works-used-to-train-ai) [[113]](https://communia-association.org/2024/12/10/a-first-look-at-the-spanish-proposal-to-introduce-ecl-for-ai-training/) [[111]](https://regulations.ai/regulations/RAI-EU-NA-TPSTCXX-2025) [[69]](https://ccb.gov/about/) [[106]](https://www.beneschlaw.com/resources/seeing-double-new-york-fashion-workers-act-creates-new-consent-requirements-for-use-of-generative-ai-tools-to-create-models-digital-replicas.html).
